original_abstract:
As artificial intelligence (AI) and natural language processing (NLP) continue to evolve, tools like ChatGPT are increasingly being used in academic and scientific writing. However, concerns regarding the accuracy of AI-generated content—specifically the phenomenon of "AI hallucination"—remain a significant barrier to their professional implementation. This study investigated the frequency and nature of fabricated information produced by ChatGPT within the context of scientific referencing.

Using an analytical study design, the researchers instructed ChatGPT to generate 50 novel medical research proposals, complete with detailed protocols and citations including Digital Object Identifiers (DOIs). A total of 178 generated references were independently verified by five researchers across multiple databases, including PubMed, Scopus, and Google Scholar. Inter-rater reliability was measured using Fleiss’ kappa and Krippendorff’s alpha to ensure the consistency of the findings.

The results revealed a substantial rate of AI hallucination. Of the 178 references analyzed, 38.7% (n=69) featured invalid or incorrect DOIs. Furthermore, 15.7% (n=28) of the cited articles were found to be entirely non-existent, appearing neither in database searches nor through DOI verification. The study also noted a tendency for the AI to cite non-peer-reviewed websites and textbooks in place of scientific journal articles.

These findings highlight serious limitations in ChatGPT’s ability to provide credible evidence for scientific manuscripts. AI hallucination poses significant ethical and legal risks, potentially compromising the integrity of research and clinical decision-making. While improving training datasets and expanding knowledge bases may mitigate these issues in the future, the study concludes that researchers must exercise extreme caution. Authors should remain personally liable for the accuracy of their work and must manually verify all AI-generated citations before publication.
humanized_abstract:
The new AI model, ChatGPT, relies on artificial intelligence and natural language processing. Lately, it has been heavily used in academic and scientific writing. However, the inaccuracy of AI-generated content, known as the “AI hallucination” issue, is still a major concern for professionals. In this study, the researcher aimed to find out the prevalence and nature of the artificial information generated by ChatGPT and verified by appropriate scientific sources.

This study employed an analytical design, in which ChatGPT was asked to generate 50 new research titles for medical studies, with respective abstracts and detailed methodologies, referencing publications containing DOIs. A total of 178 references generated by ChatGPT were verified by five researchers in the three main academic databases (PubMed, Scopus, Google Scholar). Inter-rater reliability was verified by means of Fleiss’ kappa and Krippendorff’s alpha, which assessed the level of consistency among the evaluators.

A surprisingly high frequency of what is referred to as “AI hallucination” was recorded. Among the 178 references checked, 38.7% (n=69) had invalid or different DOIs. 15.7% (n=28) of the cited articles could not be found in database searches nor confirmed with DOI. Another peculiar observation was that the AI system was more likely to reference non peer reviewed websites or textbooks rather than journal articles.

This paper discusses some of the limitations that the authors discovered when they attempted to confirm certain statements with ChatGPT, in order to include them as evidence in a scientific paper. It describes what it calls “AI hallucination,” the tendency of such programs to make up what they can’t verify in order to sound plausible and human. The authors feel that such hallucinations will become more serious over time, posing serious ethical and legal problems for science and medicine. They say that increasing and revising the training data and adding new information to the knowledge base should help to fix some of these issues in the future. In the meantime, the authors call on scientists to be vigilant. “While we understand that authors may not wish to be permanently responsible for the accuracy of the text within their research articles, we believe that, at the very least, they should bear permanent responsibility for verifying any portions of the text that have been produced with the aid of an AI.” This would require verifying and judging AI-produced sources personally and including only proven and legitimate sources in a paper, and writing or confirming and vetting all true source citations in this age of AI by hand.
